Take-home Exercise 1

Alvin Matthew

2026-05-24

Overview

Setting the Scene

  • Insurance providers often face under-performing portfolios, driven by unclear causes of poor profitability and high market volatility.
  • This project explores how visual analytics can help uncover hidden loss drivers, risk patterns and segmentation opportunities, and improved underwriting strategies.
  • Using a dataset from Mendeley Data, this analysis focuses on numerical, categorical, and temporal variables.
  • Using univariate (one variable) and bivariate (two variables) analyses
  • Plots, graphs, data cleaning, and presentation slides will be done using RStudio using R coding in Quatro Document HTML format.
  • Empty data cells are ignored based on the Missing Completely at Random (MCAR) mechanism as all variables can be considered completely independent of any observed values.
    • Our Objective: Gain deeper insights into portfolio performance and identify key factors affecting profitability and volatility in our portfolios

Distribution of Drivers’ Ages in Our Portfolio

  • Most drivers in our portfolio are aged around 40 years old (based on mode of distribution)

  • Median stands at 48 years old. This creates a distribution that is right-skewed meaning half of our clients are 48 years old and younger while the other half are older than 48 years.

Claim Frequency by Driver Age Segment

  • Based on the graph where the youngest drivers cohort is on the left, the oldest on the right, it is evident that younger drivers tend to make more claims

Relation Between Claim Severity & Age of Drivers Involved

  • It is noted that drivers aged 80 is an outlier with an extremely high average claim severity

  • Based on the weak positive linear correlation coefficient (r=0.286). This means older drivers tend to have higher claim severity but the difference compared to younger drivers is relatively small.

    * Refer to Appendix for formulas used

Distribution of Premium Paid by Policy Type

  • Basic third-party only coverage (TP) has the cheapest premium at median price of $161 with narrow deviations, meaning most clients are paying prices around the median price.

  • Comprehensive without excess (COMP_N) has the most expensive premium median price of $565 with much wider deviations, meaning even though the typical premium is highest, actual prices may vary substantially across clients.

    * Refer to Appendix for descriptions of policy types

Frequency of Claims by Policy Type

  • Despite paying the highest premium out of all the policy types, Comprehensive without excess (COMP_N) clients have the highest claim rates as well. It is also noted that COMP_N has as significantly higher number of 3+ claims than any other policy type. In fact it is almost 5 times more than the next highest number of 3+ claims.

Composition of Portfolio by Vehicle Brands (Treemap)

  • Most of the vehicles we covered are from budget brands such as Renault, Citroen, Peugeot, Volkswagen, Ford, Opel, and Seat

  • The three luxury brands that are notable are Audi, Mercedes, and BMW

  • This means that our cost of claims are leaning towards the cheaper end due to covering more budget brands than luxury brands

Total Incurred Losses Claim Type

  • Third-party injury, Third-party property damage, and First-party property damage make up the most of our claims losses.

  • These three claim types create losses amount to $65.57 million, which make up 89.33% of total losses.

Frequency & Severity Matrix of Claim Types

  • High Severity, Low Frequency: Fire and Injury

  • Low Severity, High Frequency: Windshield/Glass and Property

  • Low Severity, Low Frequency: Theft

    * Refer to Appendix for formulas used

Recommendations

  • Based on the correlation between age and claim severity, adverse claim behaviours by COMP_N clients, and loss drivers due to property damage and injury

  • Overhaul “COMP_N” underwriting rules to introduce dynamic pricing or introduce mandatory excess trigger that activates automatically after the second claim within a single policy year.

  • Refine pricing models based on age because older drivers pose more severe financial volatility than younger cohorts. Implementing a cap on claims payout or requiring mandatory health checkups or even outright not willing to insure after a certain age may help stem our losses

  • The portfolio suffers from high frequency of low severity claims such as property and glass claims. It is suggested to have a specialised repair networks and glass replacement vendors to process claims in bulk and reduce average costs.

Appendix

Formulas used

  1. \(\text{Loss Ratio} = \frac{\text{Total Incurred Losses}}{\text{Total Premium}}\)

  2. \(\text{Claim Frequency} = \frac{\text{Total Claims}}{\text{Total Exposure}}\)

  3. \(\text{Claim Severity} = \frac{\text{Total Incurred}}{\text{Total Claims}}\)

Policy Type Descriptions:

  • TP -> Basic third-party liability coverage only

  • TPG -> Third-party liability with glass coverage

  • CC -> Third-party liability combined with two or more additional coverages (theft, fire, property or glass)

  • COMP_E -> Comprehensive without excess

  • COMP_N -> Comprehensive without excess
